question: Explain the main goal of QR-factorization. What are the two main components (Q and R) and what are their key properties?
expectedPoints: The goal is to decompose a matrix A into the product of an orthogonal matrix (Q) and an upper triangular matrix (R).
Q is an orthogonal matrix, meaning its columns are orthonormal vectors (Q^T Q = I).
R is an upper triangular matrix.

question: Describe the Gram-Schmidt process and explain its role in performing QR-factorization on a matrix A with linearly independent columns.
expectedPoints: The Gram-Schmidt process is an algorithm that takes a set of linearly independent vectors and produces an orthonormal basis for their span.
It is used to create the orthogonal matrix Q, where each column is an orthonormal vector derived from the corresponding column of A.

The upper triangular matrix R is formed from the coefficients (the projections) generated during the Gram-Schmidt process, representing each original column of A as a linear combination of the new orthonormal basis.

question: Walk through the complete steps to perform QR-factorization on a given 2x2 matrix A using the Gram-Schmidt process. Explain how the entries of the resulting R matrix are calculated and why this factorization is unique if we require the diagonal entries of R to be positive.
expectedPoints: Step 1: Let the columns of A be vectors a1 and a2. Apply Gram-Schmidt: u1 = a1, e1 = u1/||u1||. Then, u2 = a2 - (a2・e1)e1, e2 = u2/||u2||. Q = [e1 | e2].
Step 2: The R matrix is upper triangular. r11 = ||a1||, r12 = a2・e1, r22 = ||u2||. The zero in R (r21) results from the orthogonalization process.

The factorization is unique for a full-rank A if we require R to have positive diagonal entries. This is because the orthonormal basis from Gram-Schmidt is uniquely determined (up to sign) by this requirement, which fixes the signs of the columns of Q.
